skill.name: changelog-automation
skill.description: Guides teams through changelog automation using Conventional Commits, Keep a Changelog, semantic-release, standard-version, git-cliff, and GitHub Actions.
skill.summary: Build repeatable changelog and release note workflows from commits, pull requests, and release metadata.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, network, weak-crypto, and token patterns, but review found they are documentation examples for changelog and release tooling rather than executable skill code. The main confirmed concern is that the playbook teaches workflows that use GitHub and npm tokens and can push tags or publish releases, so publication is acceptable with a medium-risk warning.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Release Workflow Guidance
medium_findings.description: The playbook shows GitHub Actions release jobs that use repository and npm tokens, run semantic-release, and push tags or releases. This is expected for changelog automation, but users must scope tokens carefully because copied workflows can publish packages or write to repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token references and push commands are explicit in a release workflow example. The intent appears legitimate, so this is a controlled-use risk rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Trigger Static Alerts
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are shell, npm, npx, git-cliff, and commitizen examples inside markdown fences. They are not executed by the skill itself, but users should review commands before applying them to a repository.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are contained in documentation code blocks and match the stated release-automation purpose. No evidence found that SKILL.md automatically executes them.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation and Example Repository URLs
low_findings.description: The network findings are links to public standards, documentation, and placeholder GitHub compare URLs. These are not outbound network calls from executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited URLs are markdown references and documentation links. No evidence found of data exfiltration or hidden network requests.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts correspond to changelog syntax, tables, release type text, sorting options, or release note prose. No hashing or encryption implementation was found in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found formatting and release documentation, not cryptographic code. The scanner likely matched substrings such as SHA-like or release terms without semantic context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Release Commands That Modify Repository State
dangerous_patterns.description: The playbook includes commands and workflows that generate changelogs, bump versions, push tags, and create releases. These are legitimate examples but can change production release state if copied without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and can write files, tags, or releases. They are examples in documentation, so the risk depends on user adoption.

dangerous_patterns.title: CI Secrets Used for Publishing Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: Example workflows pass GitHub and npm tokens into release tooling. This is normal for automated releases but should be limited to trusted branches and least-privilege tokens.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The secret names are present in a GitHub Actions example. They are placeholders rather than leaked secret values, but the workflow grants privileged release capabilities.

content.value_statement: Release notes often drift from actual commits and pull requests. This skill helps teams design repeatable changelog workflows using proven formats and automation tools.
actual_capabilities: Explains Keep a Changelog structure and semantic versioning rules.
Maps Conventional Commit types to changelog sections.
Provides setup patterns for commitlint, husky, standard-version, semantic-release, git-cliff, and commitizen.
Shows GitHub Actions release workflows for automated notes, tags, and releases.
Includes release note templates for public and internal audiences.
Offers best practices for commit messages and generated changelogs.
limitations: Requires reliable commit history or pull request metadata.
Does not validate secrets, permissions, or branch protection in a live repository.
Examples must be adapted for each package manager and release policy.
Generated changelogs still need human review for accuracy and sensitive details.

use_cases.title: Standardize release notes for a library
use_cases.description: Create a repeatable changelog format that converts features, fixes, and breaking changes into readable release notes.

use_cases.title: Add automated releases to CI
use_cases.description: Design a GitHub Actions workflow that generates notes, bumps versions, and publishes releases from trusted branches.

use_cases.title: Improve commit discipline across teams
use_cases.description: Introduce Conventional Commits, commitlint, and clear scopes so release automation receives clean input.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a changelog format
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a project without a formal changelog.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a changelog format for this project. Review the release process, recommend sections, and suggest a first CHANGELOG outline.

prompt_templates.title: Map commits to release sections
prompt_templates.scenario: Organize existing Conventional Commits into release notes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these commit messages and map each one to a changelog section. Flag unclear messages and suggest better commit wording.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CI release workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Create an automated release process for a repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a GitHub Actions release workflow for this repository. Include required permissions, token usage, dry-run steps, and human review points.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an existing release pipeline
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of a release automation setup.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this changelog and release automation setup. Check semantic versioning rules, token exposure risk, branch protection assumptions, and release note quality.

output_examples.input: A repository uses feat, fix, perf, and chore commits but has no changelog policy.
output: Recommended sections: Added, Fixed, Changed, Performance, and Security.
Use Conventional Commits as the input rule for generated notes.
Start with a dry-run release job before enabling tag pushes.

output_examples.input: A team wants automated GitHub releases from the main branch.
output: Use a release workflow with read-only install steps and write access only for the release job.
Store publishing tokens as CI secrets and restrict execution to trusted branches.
Review generated notes before promoting the workflow to full automation.

output_examples.input: A release has features, bug fixes, and one breaking API change.
output_examples.output: The output would group user-visible changes by section, call out the breaking change early, and include an upgrade note for affected API consumers.

best_practices: Run release tooling in dry-run mode before pushing tags or publishing packages.
Use least-privilege CI tokens and restrict release jobs to trusted branches.
Review generated notes for missing context, sensitive details, and unclear commit messages.
anti_patterns: Do not publish generated changelogs without human review.
Do not use broad write tokens for pull request builds from untrusted contributors.
Do not mix unrelated changes in one commit when automation depends on commit types.

faq.question: Does this skill generate changelogs automatically by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples for configuring tools that generate changelogs in your repository.

faq.question: Which changelog standard does it recommend?
faq.answer: It focuses on Keep a Changelog, Conventional Commits, and semantic versioning.

faq.question: Can it work with GitHub Actions?
faq.answer: Yes. The playbook includes GitHub Actions examples for release notes, version bumps, and releases.

faq.question: Does it require repository secrets?
faq.answer: Some automated publishing workflows use GitHub or npm secrets. Scope them carefully and limit release jobs to trusted branches.

faq.question: Can teams customize changelog sections?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show how commit types can map to custom sections such as Features, Fixes, and Performance.

faq.question: Should generated release notes be reviewed?
faq.answer: Yes. Human review is important for accuracy, clear wording, and removal of internal-only details.
